role_summary: Founder of the High Point, North Carolina public-relations firm B&C Associates; fundraiser and logistical aide to Martin Luther King Jr. and the SCLC; Special Assistant to President Richard Nixon (1969 to 1973) with a Black economic-development portfolio; later financier of the education of Nelson Mandela's grandchildren.
ai_reading: In this corpus the embedding reads Robert Brown (entry 106) as a movement-bridge voice rather than a single-organization figure. His highest-count semantic neighbors are Harold K. Brown (entry 55, count 161, the San Diego CORE founder), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 118, NAACP General Counsel), and the joint Simeon Booker and Moses Newson interview (entry 9, count 117, the JET magazine and Black-press team), followed by Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 99, Mississippi SNCC) and Lonnie C. King (entry 80, count 83, the Atlanta Student Movement). That the strongest tie is to another, unrelated Brown (Harold K. Brown of CORE) is an artifact worth naming for the disambiguation it forces: surname collision is not kinship, and the cluster instead spans CORE, the NAACP Legal Defense network, the Black press, Mississippi voter registration, and Atlanta sit-in organizing, none of which is Brown's own institutional home of corporate public relations. On the Nonviolence as Theology versus Armed Self-Defense axis the embedding places him at a normalized -0.55, a moderate lean toward the Armed Self-Defense pole that seats him with Norma Mtume (entry 93) and Richard Barry Sobol (entry 104) rather than with the SCLC ministers (Wyatt Tee Walker, Joseph Lowery, the Abernathy family) who anchor the opposite pole. The placement is unexpected for a self-described prayer-and-do-the-right-thing fundraiser whose testimony is saturated with Black-church language, and it likely reflects the transcript's heavy freight of Black-capitalism and economic-power vocabulary, which the axis reads as closer to Black Power praxis than to redemptive-suffering theology.
biographical_paragraph: Robert J. Brown was born February 26, 1935 in High Point, North Carolina, and raised by his grandmother, Nelly Marshall Brown [src: 1] [src: 2]. He attended Virginia Union University and North Carolina A&T State University, served as one of the first Black police officers in High Point in the mid-1950s, then worked as a federal narcotics agent in New York, testifying in the Vito Genovese organized-crime prosecution [src: 2]. He returned to North Carolina at the end of 1960 to found the public-relations firm B&C Associates, which advised corporations such as F.W. Woolworth on desegregating their operations [src: 2]. Through a 1961 introduction in Atlanta by newspaperman Stanley S. Scott, Brown met Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132) and Martin Luther King Jr., and became a fundraiser and logistical aide to King and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference [src: 1] [src: 2]. In late 1968 he joined Richard Nixon's campaign and then the White House staff, where from 1969 to 1973 he served as a Special Assistant to the President with responsibility for minority business development, equal opportunity, and outreach to historically Black colleges [src: 3] [src: 4]. After leaving the White House he rebuilt B&C and, during Nelson Mandela's imprisonment, paid personally to bring Mandela's grandchildren to the United States for schooling and visited Mandela inside Pollsmoor Prison [src: 1] [src: 2] [src: 7]. His oral history was recorded in High Point on October 1, 2013 and is held by the Library of Congress as entry 106 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Brown recalls his grandmother's answer when, as a boy in segregated High Point, he drank from the whites-only fountain in the local Woolworth and asked her why the water was separated:
interview_snippets.quote: She said, oh, yeah, it's all God's water. And she said, God's going to change it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the first corporate account that launched B&C Associates, a company whose name was then, in his words, synonymous with the lunch-counter sit-in pickets:
interview_snippets.quote: Because when I went into business in 1961, in early 1961, my first major client was the F.W. Woolworth Co.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Brown describes the Atlanta lunch where newspaperman Stanley S. Scott introduced him into King's circle:
interview_snippets.quote: And he introduced me to Rebel Rev. Wyatt Tee Walker, who was Dr. [Martin Luther] King's right hand man.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling his first private meeting with President-elect Nixon at the Pierre Hotel, where Nixon contrasted Brown's standing with that of E. Frederic Morrow under Eisenhower:
interview_snippets.quote: And he said, in Washington, access is everything.

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: On covering, from his own funds, the cost of bringing Nelson Mandela's grandchildren to the United States to attend school during Mandela's imprisonment:
interview_snippets.quote: And I paid for it personally. Not some grant I got from the foundation of from a company, from RGB personally.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ann Walker
interview_snippets.lead_in: Ann Walker, wife of SCLC chief of staff Wyatt Tee Walker, recalls her 1963 arrest near the Atlanta airport in East Point, Georgia, and who arrived from Atlanta to pay her fine:
interview_snippets.quote: And my husband saw my car. And he said, I know my wife is in jail out here. And Robert J. Brown came in and paid my $84.00 fine and got me out of jail.

movement_context: Born February 26, 1935 in High Point, North Carolina and raised by his grandparents, Brown attended North Carolina A&T, served as one of the first Black police officers in High Point, and then worked as a federal narcotics agent on cases including the Vito Genovese prosecution before returning to North Carolina in 1960 to found B&C Associates, a public-relations firm advising corporations on race and segregation policy [src: 2]. In 1968 President-elect Richard Nixon recruited him to the White House staff, and from January 1969 he served as Special Assistant to the President, the senior Black appointee with portfolio responsibility for civil rights, community relations, minority business development, equal opportunity in the military and federal agencies, and outreach to historically Black colleges and universities [src: 3] [src: 4]. Brown worked alongside Arthur Fletcher, the Assistant Secretary of Labor for Wage and Labor Standards who built the Philadelphia Plan's federal-contracting goals for minority hiring, and Leonard Garment, the White House special consultant whose civil and human rights portfolio overlapped with Brown's [src: 4] [src: 5]. Together with Garment, Brown is credited by historians of the Nixon administration with the operational momentum behind the Office of Minority Business Enterprise (OMBE), established by Nixon's March 5, 1969 executive order in the Department of Commerce, and with the federal contract set-aside mechanism that historian Dean Kotlowski argues shaped federal minority-business policy for the next two decades [src: 6]. Brown arranged the meeting between Nixon and the presidents of historically Black colleges that produced Nixon's commitment of more than $100 million in federal funds for HBCUs, and he was present in the March 25, 1971 Cabinet Room meeting between Nixon and the Congressional Black Caucus [src: 5]. Earlier in the 1960s Brown had served as a fundraiser for Martin Luther King Jr. and accompanied Coretta Scott King to Memphis the day after the King assassination to help bring her husband's body home, placing him on both sides of the post-1968 reconfiguration of Black political leadership: inside the King family network and inside the Nixon White House [src: 2] [src: 7]. He left the White House at the start of Nixon's second term in 1973 and resumed leadership of B&C Associates in High Point [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: After leaving the White House in 1973 Brown rebuilt B&C Associates (later B&C International) into one of the longest-running Black-owned consulting firms in the United States, headquartered in High Point, North Carolina, and used the firm and his personal resources to organize corporate philanthropy and to underwrite the education of Nelson Mandela's children and grandchildren in American schools during the years when Mandela was imprisoned at Pollsmoor [src: 2] [src: 7]. Brown was reportedly the only American granted an extended prison visit with Mandela, a two-hour meeting against the standard fifteen-minute window for family contact, and he served afterward as a financial counselor to the Mandela family [src: 2] [src: 7]. His 2019 memoir You Can't Go Wrong Doing Right: How a Child of Poverty Rose to the White House and Helped Change the World, published by Convergent Books (an imprint of Penguin Random House), narrates his work alongside King, Nixon, Mandela, and the corporate-philanthropy networks he organized through B&C [src: 8]. High Point University, where he served as Chairman of the Board of Trustees, established the Dr. Robert J. Brown Endowed Scholarship Fund in 2021 with $3 million in contributions from HPU and Coca-Cola Consolidated to support minority students at the institution [src: 9]. He held eleven honorary doctorates and was profiled as one of Business North Carolina's Pillars of North Carolina for his combined civic, political, and business contributions [src: 2] [src: 9].
